title: Range and standard deviation
deck: Where the measures of center ask “where,” the measures of spread ask “how wide.” The range is max minus min; the standard deviation is the typical distance from the mean. Together with center, they describe almost any data set.
objectives: Compute the range and standard deviation of a data set, distinguishing between sample and population formulas; interpret the standard deviation in context.

content.text: Two classes take the same midterm and both score a mean of 75. Class A's scores cluster tightly: every student is between 73 and 77, with most right around 75. Class B's scores are spread wide: a few in the 50s, several in the 90s, with the high and low balancing to the same mean. Center alone cannot distinguish these two distributions, because the mean is the same. To tell them apart, we need a second descriptive number: a measure of spread.

This section develops two such measures. The range is the simpler one: max minus min, the width of the gap between the largest and smallest values. The standard deviation is the more informative one: the typical distance of a value from the mean, with the same units as the data. Together, center and spread describe almost any data set well enough to support every inferential move in the rest of the chapter.
5.3.1  Range: the simplest spread

The range of a data set is the maximum value minus the minimum value. For the data set {12, 9, 15, 11, 8, 14, 10}, the maximum is 15 and the minimum is 8, so the range is 15 − 8 = 7. The range is always non-negative; it has the same units as the data; and it answers the question “how wide is the gap between the largest and smallest values?” in a single subtraction.
Definition 5.4. The range of a data set is the difference between the largest and smallest values: range = max − min. The range has the same units as the data and is always non-negative.

Margin noteThe range is extremely sensitive to outliers, because it is determined entirely by the two extreme values. A single very-high or very-low data point can move the range substantially without changing the rest of the data at all. For this reason, range is a quick first-look measure of spread, not a final summary — the standard deviation, which uses every data point in its calculation, is more informative when used alongside.
5.3.2  Standard deviation: typical distance from the mean

The standard deviation measures the typical distance of a data point from the mean of the data set. The formula is a four-step recipe: subtract the mean from every value (the deviations), square each deviation, average the squared deviations, then take the square root. The final number has the same units as the original data, which makes it directly interpretable: a standard deviation of 3 inches means the typical observation is about 3 inches above or below the mean.

content.caption: Figure 5.3. Two data sets with the same mean (75) and very different spread. The range and standard deviation are the standard ways to capture that difference in a single number. Tight Class A has a small range and small SD; spread-out Class B has both much larger.

content.text: Definition 5.5. The sample standard deviation of a data set with values x1, ···, xn and mean x̄ is s = √(Σ(xi − x̄)2 / (n − 1)). The population standard deviation uses the same formula but divides by n instead of n − 1; it is written σ. The squared standard deviation is called the variance.
5.3.3  Sample versus population

The choice of divisor — n versus n − 1 — encodes the difference between describing an entire population and estimating a population's spread from a sample. When the data set is a complete enumeration of the population (every student in the school, every patient in the trial), the divisor is n and the result is the population standard deviation σ. When the data set is a sample drawn from a larger population whose spread we wish to estimate, the divisor is n − 1 and the result is the sample standard deviation s. Most real data sets are samples, so the sample formula is the default in introductory texts and in Excel's default =STDEV.S() function. Excel offers =STDEV.P() for the population case.
Center and spread together describe almost any data set well enough for the inferential machinery of Sections 5.5 and 5.6 to take over.

Common mistakeConfusing standard deviation with variance, or forgetting the final square root. The variance is the average of the squared deviations; its units are the original units squared, which makes it awkward to interpret directly (square dollars, square pounds). The standard deviation is the square root of the variance and returns to the original units. Excel's =VAR.S() returns the variance and =STDEV.S() returns its square root; do not mix the two up.

Example 5.3. ATM transactions: range and standard deviation.

USH Financial recorded the number of transactions made at its midtown ATM each of the past 9 days: 39, 46, 37, 9, 45, 10, 46, 39, 20. Find the range and the sample standard deviation of the data set.

Solution.

Step 1. Range. The maximum is 46 (appearing twice, but value 46 counts once for the range). The minimum is 9. The range is 46 − 9 = 37.

Step 2. Mean. Sum is 39+46+37+9+45+10+46+39+20 = 291; mean is 291/9 ≈ 32.33.

Step 3. Squared deviations. For each value, compute (x − 32.33)2: approximately 44.4, 186.8, 21.8, 544.4, 161.2, 498.8, 186.8, 44.4, 151.9. Sum ≈ 1,840.5.

Step 4. Sample SD. Divide by n − 1 = 8 and take the square root: s ≈ √(1840.5/8) ≈ √230.1 ≈ 15.2.

The mean number of transactions per day is about 32, with a typical day differing from that average by about 15 transactions. The range of 37 indicates the busiest day (46) and slowest day (9) were 37 apart.

In the wildStandard deviation underwrites the entire field of quality control. A manufacturer producing cans of soda labeled “12 fluid ounces” cannot fill every can to exactly 12.000 ounces; some natural variation always exists. The mean fill is calibrated to 12, but the company also reports a small standard deviation (perhaps 0.05 ounces) to characterize how much fills typically vary. Quality control rejects cans whose fill is more than three standard deviations from the mean — an idea that connects directly to the Empirical Rule introduced in Section 5.5.
Exercises

1. Find the range of the data set 72, 68, 81, 55, 79, 64, 70, 73.

    Show answer

    Answer. 26. Maximum 81, minimum 55, range 81 − 55 = 26.

  2. Find the range of 3.2, 5.8, 1.5, 7.4, 2.9, 4.6, 6.1.

    Show answer

    Answer. 5.9. Max 7.4, min 1.5, range 7.4 − 1.5 = 5.9.

  3. True or false: the range of a data set is always at least as large as its standard deviation.

    Show answer

    Answer. True. The range is the distance between the two extreme values; the standard deviation is the typical distance from the mean, which is always less than or equal to the spread between the extremes.

  4. What are the units of the variance if the data is measured in dollars?

    Show answer

    Answer. Dollars squared. Variance is the average squared deviation, so its units are the data's units squared. Taking the square root yields the standard deviation in the original units (dollars).

  5. Two classes both have a mean test score of 80. Class X has a standard deviation of 4; Class Y has a standard deviation of 12. Which class has scores more tightly clustered around the mean?

    Show answer

    Answer. Class X. A smaller standard deviation means data values typically sit closer to the mean; the SD of 4 versus 12 indicates Class X's scores are much more tightly clustered.

  6. Which Excel function computes the sample standard deviation: =STDEV.S, =STDEV.P, or =VAR.S?

    Show answer

    Answer. =STDEV.S. The .S variant divides by n − 1 (the sample formula); .P divides by n (population). =VAR.S computes the variance (squared SD), not the SD itself.
More worked examples

content.title: Range and sample standard deviation
content.problem: For the data set **{4, 8, 6, 5, 7}**, compute the **range** and the **sample standard deviation**.
content.solution:
**Range** = max − min = 8 − 4 = **4**.

**Sample SD.** First, the mean: (4+8+6+5+7)/5 = 30/5 = 6.

Deviations from mean: (4−6, 8−6, 6−6, 5−6, 7−6) = (−2, 2, 0, −1, 1).

Squared deviations: (4, 4, 0, 1, 1). Sum: **10**.

Divide by **n − 1** (= 4): 10/4 = 2.5.

Square root: √2.5 ≈ **1.58**.

So the sample SD is about **1.58** — meaning a 'typical' value in the set is roughly 1.58 away from the mean of 6.

content.title: Same range, different spread
content.problem: Compare two sets with the same range: **A = {1, 5, 5, 5, 9}** and **B = {1, 1, 5, 9, 9}**. Which has the larger standard deviation?
content.solution:
**Range is identical for both** (9 − 1 = 8). But the *distributions inside* the range are very different.

**Set A** has most values clustered at the mean (5).

Mean = 5; squared deviations = (16, 0, 0, 0, 16); sum = 32; ÷4 = 8; SD ≈ **2.83**.

**Set B** has values pushed to the extremes.

Mean = 5; squared deviations = (16, 16, 0, 16, 16); sum = 64; ÷4 = 16; SD ≈ **4.00**.

Same range, but B's SD is about 40% larger than A's. **The range only sees the extremes; the SD measures how the entire distribution is spread.** This is why SD is the preferred measure of spread in nearly every statistical context.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Range = max − min, the simplest measure of spread. Sample standard deviation = √(Σ(x − x̄)² / (n − 1)), the standard measure of how typical values deviate from the mean. Two data sets can have identical ranges but very different SDs — because range ignores everything inside the extremes.

content.text: Looking ahead

With both center and spread defined, the descriptive toolkit is complete: any data set is summarized to first approximation by its mean (or median) and its standard deviation (or range). Section 5.4 turns from numerical summaries to visual ones, introducing the four standard data displays that translate raw numbers into shapes the eye can interpret at a glance.
5.3
